story:
Cold, damp wind combed my fur and the smell of dead fish and busted hopes hung over the docks. I should’ve pulled my coat tighter, but the chill fit the bruises. My tail thumped the warped planks while my paw settled on the new weight under my coat: not my usual snub .38, but a long .44. I hate guns. I use them fine; I just hate what they do to a room. Tonight, though, iron wasn’t a choice. It was insurance. Delilah had said Blackjack was back, and with her that kind of whisper usually meant trouble already had a pulse.

He was back.

Blackjack. Not for his fur—he got the name when he shook the city’s gambling rackets until they rained money. I’d put him down hard back when I wore a badge, ran him out of town, but never put him in a cage. He slipped away like a bad memory—until he didn’t. If he set his claws in again, the city would bleed. No case, no client, just me and a promise: find him before he rooted himself too deep.

First stop: O’Malley’s, two blocks from the river and three steps from trouble, where the light didn’t bother to visit and the music kept baring its teeth behind a grin. The bartender knew me; everyone did. That’s what happens when your face keeps score. I asked about Blackjack. He shrugged, said the deck felt different. New names at the tables. Old debts waking up. He told me to try Finn, the clockmaker on Mercer—the kind who kept his ear on the bell and one paw on a bottle.

Finn was a bent, hungry squirrel who loved gears and gossip like they shared a heartbeat. His shop smelled like oil and brass and staying late. He looked me over and said I’d lost a fight and tried to buy it back. Then he fished out a playing card: ace of spades, edges chewed to nothing. Found it in an alley, not far from a back room people had started whispering about. It felt like more than trash. Like a taunt. Like a calling card. He warned me off, then added, “And when Delilah asks you to stay tonight, don’t start in on that little house by the coast.” I told him to keep listening. There might be something in it for him. His eyes brightened the way small creatures’ eyes do when they smell a payday.

Outside, the rain thinned to a smear, and I walked under neon that made the night look honest. My thoughts drifted where they always drifted when danger circled.

Delilah.

She’d been the one to whisper it first, days ago—just his name, dropped like a lit match into my ear. And if Delilah said one thing she meant three. I’d spent the nights since chasing the trail she’d started. I found her where she wanted to be found: the Lafayette, in blue silk and fishnets, a glass of vermouth held like a secret. She asked if I was packing heat or happy to see her; I let it slide. I pushed about Blackjack. She played coy, then confessed what she always confessed: she kept tabs on dangerous types. She stroked my arm and said she didn’t want me hurt again. Blackjack had sights on more than one thing—his old rackets, and a room near the river where the cards were starting to move again. That was the lead I needed.

She asked me to stay. I wanted to. I told her about a little house by the coast, someday, far from all this noise. She heard the “but.” She always did. My magnifying glass—old, ugly, too honest—has a way of showing more than faces. I’d once looked at her through it. I don’t do that to friends anymore. Some lines feel different once you’ve crossed them.

I put my hat on, stepped back into the dripping night, and patted the .44 like a pact.

“Alright, Blackjack,” I told the sky. “Deal me in.”
